questions.q: In which Indian city is the Sabarmati Ashram located?
questions.explanation: Sabarmati Ashram is situated on the bank of the Sabarmati River in Ahmedabad, Gujarat.

questions.q: Which famous leader made the Sabarmati Ashram his home?
questions.explanation: The ashram was established and used as a residence and base by Mahatma Gandhi.

questions.q: In which Indian state is the Sabarmati Ashram?
questions.explanation: The ashram lies in Ahmedabad, which is in the state of Gujarat.

questions.q: On the bank of which river does the Sabarmati Ashram stand?
questions.explanation: The ashram is named after and located beside the Sabarmati River.

questions.q: The Sabarmati Ashram is best known as the starting point of which famous march?
questions.explanation: In 1930 Gandhi set out from Sabarmati Ashram on the Salt March to Dandi to protest the British salt tax.

questions.q: What kind of place is the Sabarmati Ashram?
options: A spiritual and community living centre
questions.explanation: It was a residential ashram, a community where Gandhi and his followers lived a life of simplicity and service.

questions.q: The coastal village of Dandi, the destination of the Salt March from Sabarmati, is in which state?
questions.explanation: Dandi is a coastal village in Gujarat where Gandhi broke the salt law in April 1930.

questions.q: What activity associated with self-reliance was promoted at the Sabarmati Ashram?
questions.explanation: Gandhi encouraged hand-spinning and khadi (homespun cloth) as part of his philosophy of self-reliance.

questions.q: The Sabarmati Ashram is also commonly known by which other name?
questions.explanation: Because of its association with Gandhi, the site is widely called the Gandhi Ashram.

questions.q: Today the Sabarmati Ashram primarily functions as what?
questions.explanation: The ashram is preserved as a national memorial and museum dedicated to Gandhi's life and work.

questions.q: In which year did Gandhi establish the ashram at its Sabarmati riverbank site?
questions.explanation: Gandhi shifted the ashram to the Sabarmati riverbank site in 1917, two years after returning to India.

questions.q: In which year did Gandhi begin the Salt March from Sabarmati Ashram?
questions.explanation: Gandhi began the Salt March on 12 March 1930, walking from the ashram toward Dandi.

questions.q: Approximately how far did Gandhi walk from Sabarmati Ashram to Dandi during the Salt March?
questions.explanation: The Salt March covered roughly 385 kilometres (about 240 miles) from the ashram to Dandi over about 24 days.

questions.q: Before settling at the Sabarmati site, Gandhi first set up his Indian ashram at which Ahmedabad locality in 1915?
questions.explanation: Gandhi first founded the Satyagraha Ashram at Kochrab in Ahmedabad in 1915 before relocating to the Sabarmati riverbank.

questions.q: What was the central guiding principle of life and struggle that Gandhi cultivated at the ashram?
questions.explanation: The ashram was a centre for satyagraha, Gandhi's method of nonviolent resistance grounded in truth.

questions.q: Gandhi reportedly vowed not to return to the Sabarmati Ashram until India achieved what?
questions.explanation: After leaving for the Salt March, Gandhi vowed not to return to the ashram until India won independence, and indeed he did not live there again.

questions.q: Which simple cottage at the ashram served as Gandhi's personal residence?
questions.explanation: Hriday Kunj was Gandhi's modest cottage at the Sabarmati Ashram, now preserved with his belongings.

questions.q: Which prominent freedom fighter and later India's first Home Minister was closely associated with the Ahmedabad-based Gandhian movement?
questions.explanation: Vallabhbhai (Sardar) Patel, a leading Gujarat-based Gandhian, was a close associate during this period and later India's first Home Minister.

questions.q: Which material and lifestyle best describes the buildings and living conditions at the ashram?
options: Simple, austere structures reflecting plain living
questions.explanation: The ashram embodied Gandhi's ideals of simple, austere living, with plain functional structures rather than ornate ones.

questions.q: The museum at the Sabarmati Ashram, which houses Gandhi memorabilia, is commonly called what?
questions.explanation: The on-site museum is known as the Gandhi Sangrahalaya, designed to display letters, photographs and Gandhi's belongings.

questions.q: What was a key economic message Gandhi promoted from the ashram against British rule?
options: Boycotting foreign cloth and using swadeshi goods
questions.explanation: Gandhi promoted swadeshi, urging Indians to boycott foreign cloth and use indigenous, homespun khadi.

questions.q: The Sabarmati Ashram served as the nerve centre for which broader 1930 national campaign?
questions.explanation: The Salt March launched from the ashram inaugurated the Civil Disobedience Movement of 1930.

questions.q: Roughly how many followers initially set out with Gandhi from the ashram on the Salt March on 12 March 1930?
questions.explanation: Gandhi began the march with about 78 to 80 chosen ashram inmates, a number that swelled into a large crowd along the way.

questions.q: What was the original name Gandhi gave to the ashram he founded in Ahmedabad?
questions.explanation: Gandhi named it the Satyagraha Ashram, reflecting its purpose as a centre for truth-based nonviolent resistance.

questions.q: Which two earlier settlements founded by Gandhi in South Africa were models for the Sabarmati Ashram community?
questions.explanation: Gandhi's South African communities, the Phoenix Settlement and Tolstoy Farm, were precursors that shaped the Sabarmati Ashram's communal life.

questions.q: On approximately what date did Gandhi reach Dandi and break the salt law after marching from the ashram?
questions.explanation: Gandhi reached Dandi and ceremonially broke the salt law on 6 April 1930, after about 24 days of marching.

questions.q: The architect who later designed the Gandhi Sangrahalaya museum building at the ashram (opened in 1963) was which renowned Indian architect?
questions.explanation: The Gandhi Sangrahalaya at Sabarmati was designed by the noted Indian architect Charles Correa, opening in 1963.

questions.q: After Gandhi left the ashram in 1930, what notably happened to the Sabarmati Ashram for a period?
options: It was converted into a palace
Gandhi formally disbanded it and it was later revived as a memorial
questions.explanation: Gandhi disbanded the ashram around 1933 amid the freedom struggle, and it was later preserved and revived as a national memorial.

questions.q: Which of these was a core vow or discipline practised by inmates of Gandhi's Sabarmati Ashram?
options: Observance of truth, nonviolence and celibacy among the ashram vows
questions.explanation: Ashram inmates took vows including truth (satya), nonviolence (ahimsa) and celibacy (brahmacharya) as part of disciplined communal life.

questions.q: Gandhi's campaign for the upliftment of the so-called untouchables, whom he called by which name, was actively promoted from the ashram?
questions.explanation: Gandhi called the marginalized untouchable communities 'Harijan' (children of God) and championed their upliftment from the ashram.
